data.title: Cisco shows off new software to connect quantum computing cloud
data.description:
Cisco Systems (NASDAQ:CSCO) announced new software on Thursday that is aimed at connecting a quantum computing cloud.

The software prototypes, which will become live next week at Cisco's Quantum Summit, include: a quantum compiler, which enables quantum algorithms to run across multiple networked processors; a quantum alert, which provides security with guarantees from physics; and a quantum sync, a decision coordination application demo that uses entanglement to enable correlated decision-making across distributed locations for classical use cases.

“At Cisco, we have always believed in a full-stack approach to building robust, high-performing systems,” Cisco executive Vijoy Pandey wrote in a blog post. “For classical networking, we design and build this stack from the ground up—custom silicon, integrated hardware systems, and all the layers of software that control and manage everything. It’s the philosophy that enabled us to pioneer many of the foundational technologies of the classical internet, from the core of the data center to the edge of wireless networks, and all the connectivity in between.”

Cisco shares rose fractionally in late day trading on Thursday.

data.title: FDA removes REMS program for Sanofi thyroid cancer drug Caprelsa
data.description:
• The FDA has withdrawn the Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies (REMS) program for Sanofi's (NASDAQ:SNY) medullary thyroid cancer treatment Caprelsa (vandetanib).
  • The agency said that since the drug was approved in 2011, there have been no reports of heart rhythm issues or unexplained sudden deaths associated with the med in US patients.
  • The FDA added that clinical data also found no patterns indicating heart rhythm problems. 
  • Caprelsa was developed by Genzyme, which was acquired by Sanofi in 2011 for $20.1B.

data.title: RFK Jr. reportedly weighs listing autism for vaccine compensation
data.description:
Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is considering plans to add certain autism symptoms to a list of adverse events that require his department to compensate under the national vaccine injury program, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday, citing an advisor.

As HHS Secretary, Kennedy, a well-known vaccine skeptic, has the authority to determine which injuries should be compensated under the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program.

Established in the 1980s, the program maintains a fund to offer compensation for people who experience injuries from covered vaccines. It was implemented after lawsuits related to vaccine injury claims jeopardized U.S. vaccine availability and threatened the country’s immunization rates.

On Monday, announcing a range of measures to address autism in children, President Donald Trump, flanked by RFK Jr. and Medicare Administrator Mehmet Oz, suggested a link between the neurodevelopmental disorder and vaccines.

"There are certain groups of people that don't take vaccines and don't take any pills that have no autism," Trump said, triggering backlash from the medical community over his efforts to peddle claims widely debunked in scientific studies.

Leading vaccine makers: Pfizer (NYSE:PFE)/ BioNTech (BNTX), Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA), Novavax (NVAX), GSK (NYSE:GSK), Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY)

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Lithium Americas extends big gains despite TD Cowen downgrade
data.description:
Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) +18.1% in Thursday's trading to a two-year intraday high $7.53, adding to its 95% surge in the previous session following reports that the Trump administration is seeking an equity stake in the company as it renegotiates terms of a $2.3 billion U.S. government loan.

The dizzying gains are looking frothy to TD Cowen's David Deckelbaum, who downgraded Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) to Hold from Buy as shares have breached his price target and net asset value of $5/share, saying the situation "remains iterative, but for now, we see little rationale for a significantly enhanced economic model with current anchor tenant, GM, providing an undisclosed price floor."

However, the analyst believes Lithium Americas (LAC) stands to be a major producer of battery grade lithium carbonate, particularly in North America as Thacker Pass comes online, and will benefit from growing demand for electric vehicles and the general electrification trend.

The recent reports point towards a reassessment of GM's existing offtake agreement with a push for a take-or-pay structure to ensure sales, but Deckelbaum believes the market already had priced in minimally 100% of Phase 1 sales as shares currently imply pricing in excess of $20K/tonne, well above the current ~$10.3k/tonne spot and in light of GM's nearly $950M investment to date.

data.title: Eli Lilly wins approval of imlunestrant for breast cancer
data.description:
• The U.S. FDA has approved Eli Lilly's (NYSE:LLY) imlunestrant, under the brand name Inluriyo, as a second-line therapy for ER+,  HER2–, and ESR1-mutated advanced or metastatic breast cancer.
  • The oral estrogen receptor antagonist won approval based on results from the phase 3 EMBER-3 trial which found that Inluriyo cut the risk of progression or death by 38% compared to endocrine therapy. 
  • In patients with ESR1-mutated breast cancer, those on Inluriyo had a median progression-free survival of 5.5 months  vs. 3.8 months with fulvestrant or exemestane. 
  • Inluriyo is in another phase 3 trial examining the treatment in an adjuvant setting for individuals with ER+, HER2– early breast cancer.

data.title: JPMorgan says Fed's monetary policy committee 'will remain unmoved' by Governor Miran
data.description:
Newly appointed Federal Reserve Governor Stephen Miran in a speech this week called for a federal funds rate almost 2 percentage points lower than current policy. After taking a look at his arguments, JPMorgan found some of them "questionable, others incomplete, and almost none persuasive." 

"Barring a radical change in the composition of the FOMC, we don’t see his case winning the day," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said on Thursday, referring to the Federal Open Market Committee.

"We investigate Miran’s arguments, and find that they are indeed incredible. However, we do not find them persuasive. We also strongly doubt they will convince others on the FOMC. As such, we are leaving intact our call for rates to be lowered in measured 25bp increments until reaching 3.25-3.5% early next year," Feroli added. 

The Fed last week cut its key policy rate by 25 basis points, easing monetary policy for the first time in nearly nine years. Governor Miran voted against the action, instead preferring an even larger 50 basis point cut.

Miran, President Donald Trump's top economic adviser, was confirmed to the Fed's Board of Governors last week. He is filling in the seat vacated by Adriana Kugler in August.

Miran on Monday, speaking at the Economic Club of New York, said he believed the "appropriate fed funds rate" was "in the mid-2 percent area." The current rate is 4.00%-4.25%. One of the chief arguments in his speech was that the neutral rate of interest, also known as the r*, is 1 to 1.2 percentage points lower than where the markets and his colleagues on the FOMC think it is, due to policy changes made this year. 

"Miran’s speech presented the dovish side of a number of issues regarding the monetary policy debate. A central part of his case was the importance of a lower estimate for the real neutral interest rate, or r*," JPMorgan's Feroli said. 

Calling Miran's reasoning "selective and motivated," Feroli believes that it is "unlikely to persuade a committee that dissects every argument."

Miran in his speech also offered an optimistic view on rental inflation, contending that declining inflation in rents takes time to show up in the official inflation numbers.

"Almost all of Miran’s inflation discussion was occupied by a discussion of rental inflation. As for the other components, he assumes they will 'continue at their current run rates.' As Milton Friedman warned many years ago, looking at inflation exclusive of certain prices risks conflating relative price changes with inflation, a phenomenon of the overall price level," Feroli said. 

"This only scratches the surface of the critique of Miran’s arguments. Picking apart everything he 'threw at the wall' would take time. But the relevant point here is that the Fed governors, presidents, and their staffs have the time and expertise to pick apart these arguments," he said. 

"While Miran will have more opportunities to try to convince the Committee of the merits of his arguments, we are skeptical that the members will find them convincing, particularly when those arguments elide upside inflation risks," Feroli added.

The Fed has remained firmly in Wall Street's spotlight since its interest rate decision last week. Aside from Miran, traders have heard about the Fed's decision from several other policymakers over the past few days. Stocks were lower on Thursday. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: Trump says tariff revenue will fund farmer relief
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Thursday announced that his administration plans to channel money collected from tariffs into aid for struggling U.S. farmers, who have borne much of the cost of his trade battles.

“We’re going to take some of that tariff money that we’ve made, we’re going to give it to our farmers, who are — for a little while — going to be hurt until it kicks in, the tariffs kick in to their benefit,” Trump said.

Farm communities, a core part of Trump’s political base in 2024, have seen export markets shrink during his second term as trading partners retaliated, while federal support programs were pared back.

Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins said this week that relief could be rolled out “in the next couple of weeks.”

Trump’s remarks gave a clearer indication of how the program might be funded. But relying on tariff revenue carries legal risks: lower courts have ruled that many of the president’s duties, imposed under emergency authority, were unlawful. If the Supreme Court upholds those rulings, Washington could be forced to refund tens of billions of dollars to importers.

Despite the uncertainty, farmers have been clamoring for assistance. Crop prices have remained weak while input costs for seed, fertilizer and equipment have climbed, leaving razor-thin margins.

China’s retaliatory measures have deepened the strain. Beijing, the world’s largest buyer of soybeans, has yet to order a single shipment of U.S. supply this season, adding to fears as harvest season moves ahead.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Current market levels have historically led to ‘weaker than average’ short-term returns – Bespoke co-founder
data.description:
Bespoke Investment Group co-founder Paul Hickey cautioned about short-term market conditions.

“In the shorter term, though, I think we’ve gotten very extended,” Hickey warned during a CNBC interview, noting that tech sectors were showing the same extreme levels of overextension to the upside that they showed to the downside in April.

“We’ve been at overbought levels on over 90% of trading days over the last four months for the Nasdaq,” he said.

He explained that historically, such extreme conditions have led to “weaker than average” short-term returns, concluding that “you do need to see some sort of pause in the market here” to prevent instability similar to what occurred in 1999.

On the other hand, Joe Moglia, FG Nexus co-founder and executive advisor, said today’s market is fundamentally different from the dot-com bubble of 2000-2002, citing the strength of current tech companies.

“I think this is still the beginning. This is 1994, 1995 as far as back then goes for today,” he said, emphasizing that unlike the speculative companies of the late 1990s, today’s tech giants (NVDA), (MSFT), (AAPL), (GOOGL) have “incredible balance sheets.”

Moglia highlighted that during the dot-com era, many companies rushing to market weren’t financially sound.

“The dot-com bubble, everybody was coming to market. A lot of those companies weren’t very, very good companies,” he noted, contrasting with today’s “Magnificent Seven” tech leaders, which he described as “really, really solid companies.”

data.title: Uranium Energy downgraded at BMO on valuation after strong gains
data.description:
Uranium Energy (NYSE:UEC) -1.4% in Thursday's trading as BMO Capital downgraded shares to Market Perform from Outperform with a $14 price target, after more than tripling since early April even as earnings are not yet material and sales are minimal.

With exposure to spot uranium prices and several growth projects, shareholders could benefit from higher uranium prices, the momentum behind nuclear energy and the high liquidity of the stock," BMO's Alexander Pearce wrote, but "the company appears fairly priced vs. its closest peers at this stage" following the stock's strong gains.

Sentiment in the sector continues to improve, particularly for U.S. domestic uranium producers, with Uranium Energy (NYSE:UEC) offering attractive mid-term growth to meet growing demand, and the stock has a number of near-term catalysts including potentially positive U.S. policy developments, the analyst said.

Reviewing just-released FY 2025 results, Uranium Energy (UEC) reported an earnings loss of $0.16/share, an adjusted EBITDA loss of $70 million, and $67 million in revenues; on the operational front, FY 2025 U3O8 production totaled 26,400 lbs U3O8.

data.title: HHS sends proposed drug pricing rule to White House for review
data.description:
The White House announced on Thursday that a proposed drug pricing rule drafted by the Department of Health & Human Services (HHS) and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) is currently pending its review.

An economically significant proposed rule titled “Global Benchmark for Efficient Drug Pricing (GLOBE) Model” has been received by the White House’s Office of Management and Budget, according to a notice on the agency’s website.

The development marks the U.S. government’s latest effort to lower U.S. drug prices to the level of other developed nations in accordance with President Donald Trump’s most-favored-nation (MFN) pricing policy.

In May, Trump signed an executive order reviving the rule, which he initially introduced in 2020 but was later blocked by federal courts and rescinded by the then-Biden administration.

The proposed rule comes days ahead of a Sept. 29 deadline set by Trump for major pharmaceutical companies to lower U.S. drug prices in line with the MFN policy. He sends letters to the CEOs of 17 leading drugmakers, including Eli Lilly (LLY), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), and Merck (NYSE:MRK), issuing a 60-day ultimatum to comply with the rule.

Other U.S. pharma majors to receive the letters included Gilead (GILD), Bristol Myers (BMY), J&J (NYSE:JNJ), Regeneron (REGN), Amgen (AMGN), and AbbVie (ABBV). European drugmakers Merck KGaA (OTCPK:MKGAF), Sanofi (SNY), GSK (NYSE:GSK), AstraZeneca (AZN), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), and Novartis (NVS) were also part of the group.
